issues.description: Spoken 'sine of three x squared' is ambiguous: a listener cannot distinguish sin(3x squared) from sin((3x) squared). The stated inside derivative 'six x' is only correct for sin(3x^2); if a learner hears (3x)^2=9x^2 the inside derivative would be 18x. This ambiguity is especially hazardous because it sits one line before the chain-rule trap about exactly this nesting.
issues.fix: Disambiguate verbally and on-screen, e.g. 'sine of the quantity three-x-squared, that is sine of 3-x-to-the-second-power' and show sin(3x^2) explicitly so the inside g(x)=3x^2, g'(x)=6x reading is unambiguous.

issues.description: Conflates 'one-to-one' with 'monotonic' as if equivalent ('f has to be one-to-one — monotonic on the interval'). One-to-one does not require monotonicity in general; equating them builds a wrong mental model that a learner may carry into other units.
issues.fix: State it as an implication, not an equivalence: 'f must be one-to-one; the easiest sufficient condition is that f is strictly monotonic (increasing or decreasing) on the interval.'

issues.description: Formulas are presented with bare x (1/sqrt(1-x^2), etc.) and the chain-rule reminder 'if the argument is u of x, chain in u prime' is appended only as a final clause. A learner is likely to memorize the bare-x form and forget to multiply by u'(x) for arcsin(u), arctan(u), repeating the very chain-rule trap warned about in 07.
issues.fix: Show at least one worked u-argument example on screen, e.g. d/dx arctan(3x) = 3/(1+9x^2), so the u' factor is anchored, not just mentioned.

issues.description: States sin^-1(x) 'is always the inverse function unless told otherwise,' but does not warn that the SAME negative-one superscript on the OUTPUT, e.g. sin^2(x) vs (sin x)^-1 = csc x, and that (sin x)^-1 with explicit parentheses genuinely IS the reciprocal. The blanket rule could mislead a learner into reading (sin x)^-1 as arcsin too.
issues.fix: Add the contrast explicitly: 'sin^-1 x means arcsin x, but (sin x)^-1 with the parentheses around sin x means the reciprocal, cosecant. The placement of the exponent and parentheses decides it.'

notes: Mathematically accurate throughout (slopes, implicit answers, second-derivative result all check out), but several spoken-notation ambiguities and a one-to-one/monotonic conflation risk seeding chain-rule and notation misconceptions; all fixable without rework.
